excerpts: On September 23, 2025, Walz launched _Find My Parking Cops_ , a map that deduced the near real-time locations and routes of San Francisco parking-enforcement officers by scraping predictable citation IDs from the city's online payment portal. _Wired_ detailed the method and reported that the site went viral before San Francisco MTA officials altered its systems four hours later, disabling the feed. [[ 3 ]]() [[ 13 ]]() [[ 14 ]]() Local outlets noted the shutdown and the public debate over enforcement transparency. [[ 14 ]]() [[ 15 ]]()

In November 2025, Walz and web developer Luke Igel launched _Jmail_ , a browser-based archive of public emails released under the Epstein Files Transparency Act . The site presents the documents through a Gmail -style interface as if viewed from Jeffrey Epstein 's personal inbox. Walz announced the project on Twitter , writing that "we cloned Gmail, except you're logged in as Epstein and can see his emails." [[ 16 ]]() The site incorporates "Jemini," an AI search tool, and uses Gemini for optical character recognition to extract text from document scans. [[ 17 ]]() The project took five hours to develop and attracted an estimated 18.4 million visits by late November 2025. [[ 18 ]]() Walz and Igel later expanded the project with JPhotos, an image database; JFlights, a flight tracking site for Epstein's travel; Jamazon, which tracks his Amazon") orders; Jacebook, his Facebook account; Jmessage, his iMessages; Jotify, his music ('Spotify'); and Jdrive, his Google Drive.

Between 2009 and 2012, iPhones had a built-in "Send to YouTube" button in the Photos app. Many of these uploads kept their default IMG_XXXX filenames, creating a time capsule of raw, unedited moments from random lives.

content.executive_summary: Riley Walz is a San Francisco-based software engineer and internet artist known for creating a series of viral, data-driven web projects that often serve as social commentary. His work is characterized by a distinct professional pattern of rapid, short-cycle development, typically building and launching projects like 'Bop Spotter' and 'Find My Parking Cops' over a weekend. A recurring theme across his portfolio is the exploration and visualization of publicly accessible, yet often overlooked, data. This includes reverse-engineering the SFMTA's parking ticket system, scraping YouTube for videos with default filenames ('IMG_0001'), and setting up a 24/7 Shazam-powered microphone to capture the 'vibes' of the Mission District ('Bop Spotter'). His work consistently touches upon themes of nostalgia, surveillance, data ethics, and the aesthetics of the early internet. Walz's public persona, as projected through his own statements and media interviews, is that of a curiosity-driven maker who resists formal labels. He describes himself as 'just a normal guy who knows something about technology and likes seeing amusing things' and prefers the simple title of 'engineer' over more pretentious labels like 'artist.' He frames his provocative projects not as political statements but as 'cool' experiments to see what happens, a stance that positions him as a playful tinkerer or 'jester' operating within the tech landscape.

content.idea_generation_analysis:
Riley Walz's approach to idea generation is rooted in a blend of personal experience, whimsical curiosity, and external inspiration, often leading to projects that explore everyday phenomena through a technological lens. Many of his ideas spring from direct, personal observations or needs. For his early project, Routeshuffle, the motivation was entirely personal: "I'm Riley, a distance runner from New York. I made Routeshuffle to help with my summer training in high school." Similarly, the 'Find My Parking Cops' app, despite him not owning a car, was inspired by his social circle: "I actually don’t have a car," he told Mission Local, "But my friends do, and I know that parking tickets are kind of notorious in this city." This demonstrates a pattern of solving problems or exploring annoyances present in his immediate environment.

A significant driver for Walz is simple, open-ended curiosity. His projects often begin with the question, "Wouldn't it be cool if...?" For 'Bop Spotter,' which monitors music in the Mission District, he explained to KQED, "So I thought it would be cool to Shazam from one fixed place and just see the results over time. I honestly just thought it would be cool to hear what people are playing." This curiosity extends to unearthing hidden digital artifacts, as with his project IMG_0001, which collects obscure YouTube videos. He was inspired by a blog post and thought, "it'd be cool to get the ones that are zero views that people hadn't seen before." This method highlights a preference for exploration over predetermined outcomes.

Walz also emphasizes the importance of acting on these ideas, viewing idea generation as a muscle that requires exercise. In a quote reported by eWeek, he stressed, “You have to follow through on your ideas because if you don’t, you might stop having them. It’s like a muscle you have to keep using.” This philosophy connects his ideation directly to his rapid development process, suggesting that for him, an idea is only valuable once it is pursued and brought to life.
content.process_analysis:
Riley Walz's work process is defined by an exceptionally rapid, iterative, and public-facing development cycle. He is known for turning ideas into functional prototypes over very short timeframes, often within a single weekend. He described this pattern to Wired, stating, "I start them over the course of the weekend and just put them out there and see what happens." This approach prioritizes speed and experimentation over prolonged development. The creation of his nostalgic video-curation site, IMG_0001, exemplifies this speed; he noted the process took "maybe four or five hours total from when I saw the blog post. The website was pretty much done over the course of a couple days."

This 'weekend hack' model is a consistent theme. His 'Find My Parking Cops' tool was developed "in my free time on the weekends the last few weeks to make it happen." Similarly, the 'Bop Spotter' installation was a low-cost, fast-turnaround project, reportedly costing about $100 and taking a "couple weekends" to set up. This lean methodology allows him to maintain a high output of creative experiments without getting bogged down in complex, long-term projects.

His process is also deeply technical and hands-on, often involving clever reverse-engineering of existing systems. For the parking ticket app, he didn't just use a public API; he discovered a hidden pattern in the data. He explained to Wired, “I figured out that the ticket numbers are predictable, which means I can efficiently scrape them.” He even detailed the specific algorithm on his blog, which was quoted in the article: “It seems each possible ticket number follows a pattern: add 11, except add 4 if the last digit is 6. So, no ticket can end in 7, 8, or 9.” This combination of high-level, rapid prototyping with low-level data sleuthing is a hallmark of his engineering process.
content.taste_analysis:
Riley Walz's aesthetic and curatorial taste is characterized by a deep appreciation for nostalgia, early-internet authenticity, and the unique cultural signals embedded in everyday life. His work often functions as a form of digital archaeology, unearthing and re-presenting artifacts from a less polished era of the web. The New Yorker profile highlights this, noting that his projects "feel like memories." This is most evident in IMG_0001, a project that curates millions of YouTube videos with default filenames. Walz's fascination with these clips goes beyond simple data collection; he told the magazine, “I was thinking maybe one in ten would be interesting, but all of them are,” revealing a curatorial eye for the raw, unedited moments of life captured in these forgotten videos.

His taste also extends to the ambient culture of physical spaces. For 'Bop Spotter,' his choice of San Francisco's Mission District was deliberate. He described it to KQED as “such a unique neighborhood with so many cultures and so much depth.” The project itself, which he describes on his website as being about "catching vibes" rather than "catching criminals," showcases a sensibility geared towards passive, artistic observation of culture. He finds value in the ambient soundtrack of a neighborhood, a form of cultural data that is often overlooked.

Furthermore, Walz shows an appreciation for clever, system-aware pranks and art. A 2020 post on his X/Twitter account admiringly shared a story about an artist who created virtual traffic jams on Google Maps using a wagon full of phones. This indicates a taste for work that playfully subverts technological systems to make a point or create an unexpected outcome, a quality that is reflected in his own provocative projects like 'Find My Parking Cops.'
content.motivation_analysis:
Riley Walz's stated motivations are consistently centered on curiosity, personal amusement, and the intrinsic satisfaction of creating something 'cool' or interesting, rather than commercial gain or overt political activism. He repeatedly frames his work as experiments born from a desire to see what is possible with technology and data. In an interview with KQED about his 'Bop Spotter' project, he summarized his ethos: “I’m just a normal guy who knows something about technology and likes seeing amusing things... I had an idea for Bop Spotter and just wanted to see what would happen.” This sentiment positions him as a playful explorer and tinkerer.

He actively distances himself from grander or more political intentions, even when his projects touch on sensitive topics like surveillance or civic data. Regarding the 'Find My Parking Cops' app, which could be seen as an anti-authority tool, he clarified his stance to ABC7: "I'm not trying to make a grand political statement. I think this is really cool data to look at." He further told the SF Standard, "I'm not 'pro' parking cop. I'm not 'anti' parking cop. It's just data I was able to unearth, and I thought it would be cool to visualize it." This consistent framing suggests his primary driver is the intellectual and creative challenge of working with data, not a specific political agenda.

This motivation to simply 'see something cool' and share it is a core part of his identity. He explained this to Wired, saying, "I feel like I'm just a guy who knows something about technology who just wants to see something cool, and I just share it with people to see what they think." This indicates that the public release of his projects is an extension of his curiosity—the reaction is part of the experiment. His motivation is not to build a product or a company, but to satisfy an engineering and creative impulse and observe the outcome.
content.labels_analysis:
Riley Walz demonstrates a clear preference for simple, functional professional labels while actively resisting more interpretive or grandiose titles like 'artist' or 'prankster.' His identity is firmly grounded in the practice of building and creating with technology. When asked by Wired how he would describe his job, his response was succinct and definitive: “Just engineer is fine.” This preference for the 'engineer' label highlights his focus on the technical craft and problem-solving aspects of his work over any artistic or political interpretation.

He is uncomfortable with labels that he feels are pretentious or misrepresent his motivations. The New Yorker profile noted that he “considers the word art ‘too pretentious’” to describe his work. This rejection of the 'artist' label aligns with his stated motivation of simply creating 'cool' things out of curiosity, rather than producing formal artworks for the art world. He reiterated this sentiment to Wired, stating, "I don't really like labels like that," referring to more complex or interpretive titles.

Despite his resistance, media coverage often assigns him more colorful labels. He has been called the "jester of Silicon Valley" and a "tech prankster" by outlets like eWeek and Hoodline. While his projects often have a playful or provocative nature that invites such descriptions, his own words indicate a discomfort with these monikers. He sees himself not as a prankster with an agenda, but as an engineer sharing interesting experiments, pushing back against labels that imply a specific intent beyond technical curiosity.
content.ethics_analysis:
Riley Walz's work frequently engages with complex ethical issues, particularly concerning data privacy, public surveillance, and consent, though his public statements on these topics are often more provocative and descriptive than deeply philosophical. He demonstrates a clear awareness of the ethical gray areas his projects inhabit, often addressing them head-on with a disarming or artistic framing. On the project page for 'Bop Spotter,' a device that constantly listens to and Shazams street music, he writes with stark self-awareness: "This is culture surveillance. No one notices, no one consents. But it's not about catching criminals. It's about catching vibes." This statement acknowledges the lack of consent inherent in the project while reframing its purpose from punitive surveillance to aesthetic curation.

When his projects have more direct real-world implications, Walz tends to downplay any malicious or anti-authoritarian intent. After releasing the 'Find My Parking Cops' app, which tracked the location of parking enforcement officers, he told ABC7, "I'm not out to get the parking cops. I respect the cops and stuff." This serves to position the project as a neutral data experiment rather than a tool for evasion, despite its obvious utility for that purpose.

He also acknowledges the inherent flaws and biases in data-driven systems he creates. On the page for 'LooksMapping,' a project that used AI to rate attractiveness, he wrote, "The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed. But we judge places by the people who go there... A mirror held up to our collective vanity." This shows a willingness to confront the ethical shortcomings of his own creations. His project 'Jmail,' which cloned the Gmail interface to display Jeffrey Epstein's publicly released emails, is another example of his provocative approach, using a familiar and personal format to present sensitive public records, thereby raising questions about data presentation and voyeurism. Overall, his ethical stance appears to be one of a provocateur who uses engineering to surface uncomfortable questions about data and privacy in the digital age, rather than providing clear answers.
content.data_analysis:
Riley Walz's work is fundamentally driven by a deep fascination with data—specifically, finding, scraping, reverse-engineering, and visualizing public datasets in novel ways. He explicitly identifies with this passion, telling a reporter for ABC7, "I'm a huge data nerd." This identity is borne out by a consistent pattern of projects that transform raw, often obscure, data into interactive public experiences. His skill lies not just in analyzing data, but in acquiring it through clever technical means.

His most famous projects are showcases of his data acquisition skills. For 'Find My Parking Cops,' he didn't use a sanctioned API but instead reverse-engineered the system. He announced on X/Twitter: "I reverse engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system. I can see every ticket seconds after it's written." He later explained the technical breakthrough to Wired: “I figured out that the ticket numbers are predictable, which means I can efficiently scrape them.” This demonstrates a sophisticated ability to find and exploit patterns in seemingly opaque systems. This pattern is repeated in other projects, such as scraping Amtrak data to show train occupancy and creating a bot that "crawled YouTube and found 5 million" videos for his IMG_0001 project.

For Walz, the data itself is the main attraction. He sees inherent value and interest in unearthing and visualizing information, often independent of any political or practical goal. He described the parking ticket data as "just data I was able to unearth, and I thought it would be cool to visualize it." This perspective frames data not as a means to an end, but as an end in itself—a source of endless curiosity and creative material. His 'Bop Spotter' project, designed to "log some trends" in music over years, further underscores his interest in long-term, ambient data collection as a form of cultural observation.
content.nostalgia_analysis:
Nostalgia is a powerful and recurring theme that permeates Riley Walz's work, influencing both his choice of subject matter and his aesthetic presentation. He is often framed as a curator of the internet's recent past, creating experiences that evoke a sense of a bygone digital era. A Wired profile aptly titled him "the guy behind the most nostalgic sites on the internet," capturing this central aspect of his public persona. His projects often serve as portals to a rawer, less commercialized web.

The most direct manifestation of this theme is his project IMG_0001, which collects and displays millions of YouTube videos with default camera filenames like 'IMG_0001.MOV'. These are unedited, often personal clips from the early days of smartphones—digital artifacts that are effectively lost in the noise of the modern internet. A profile in The New Yorker captured the essence of this project's appeal, quoting Walz's reflection on the videos: “They feel like memories.” This statement reveals that his interest is not merely technical; it is deeply affective, tied to the emotional resonance of these forgotten digital moments. He is not just archiving data; he is curating feelings of nostalgia for a shared, more innocent technological past.

This nostalgic sensibility is also present in the aesthetic choices of his websites and the types of online phenomena he is drawn to. His work often has a simple, unadorned quality that recalls early web design. This deliberate invocation of early-internet aesthetics, combined with his focus on archival and curation, positions nostalgia as a core tenet of his artistic and engineering practice.
content.engineering_analysis:
Riley Walz's professional identity is, first and foremost, that of an engineer. When asked by Wired to describe his job, he provided a clear and concise answer that cuts through any other labels the public or media might apply: “Just engineer is fine.” This self-identification is consistently supported by the nature of his work, which is deeply rooted in technical problem-solving, system analysis, and software development. His projects are not just concepts; they are functional, engineered web experiences.

His engineering practice is characterized by a hands-on, often unconventional, approach to accessing and manipulating data. He demonstrates a strong capability in reverse-engineering systems to achieve his goals. For his 'Find My Parking Cops' project, he didn't rely on a public API but instead, as he stated, "reverse engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system." He discovered and exploited a predictable pattern in the ticket numbers, a classic engineering feat of finding order in a seemingly chaotic system. This skill is also evident in his creation of a bot to crawl YouTube for his IMG_0001 project and his work scraping Amtrak's reservation inventory to infer train capacity.

His work is often described as creating "novel web experiences that make complex systems visible and interactive." This points to an engineering philosophy that values not just functionality but also user-facing visualization and interaction. He applies his skills to build tools and dashboards that make data accessible and engaging to a broad audience. His hiring by OpenAI, a leading AI research and engineering organization, serves as further validation of his skills and his identity as an engineer operating at a high level, applying his experimental and data-driven approach to cutting-edge technology.
content.launch_behavior_analysis:
Riley Walz's launch strategy is characterized by speed, informality, and a philosophy of public experimentation. He eschews long, private development cycles in favor of rapidly building a prototype and releasing it to the world to observe the reaction. He articulated this approach clearly in a Wired interview: “I start them over the course of the weekend and just put them out there and see what happens.” This behavior treats the launch not as a polished, final event, but as an integral part of the creative process itself, where public interaction and unforeseen consequences are part of the experiment.

The 'Find My Parking Cops' project is a quintessential example of his launch behavior. After developing the tool, he announced it publicly on his X/Twitter account with a provocative and catchy message: “I reverse engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system... So I made a website. Find My Friends? AVOID THE PARKING COPS.” This informal, direct-to-audience announcement on social media is his typical method. The project went viral almost immediately, demonstrating the effectiveness of this strategy. The subsequent shutdown of the data source by the SFMTA just four hours after launch is also part of this pattern; his launches are often so disruptive or novel that they provoke immediate reactions from the systems they interact with. He documents this on the project page, noting, "This site only worked for 4 hours."

This pattern of quick, often weekend-built projects followed by immediate public release on his personal site (walzr.com) and social media (@rtwlz) is consistent across his portfolio. Whether it's a nostalgic video archive like IMG_0001 or a provocative data tool like Jmail, the behavior is the same: build it fast, launch it publicly, and see what happens.
content.professional_pattern_implications:
Synthesizing Riley Walz's public statements and project history reveals a consistent professional pattern with clear implications for modeling his behavior. 

**Decision-Making Heuristics:** Walz's primary heuristic for project selection is driven by personal curiosity and the potential for an 'amusing' or 'cool' outcome, rather than commercial viability or strategic alignment. He is motivated by the challenge of unearthing and visualizing data, as stated in an eWeek article: “You have to follow through on your ideas because if you don’t, you might stop having them. It’s like a muscle you have to keep using.” His decisions are biased towards action and rapid execution; if an idea can be prototyped over a weekend, it is likely to be pursued. He displays a high tolerance for risk and impermanence, readily launching projects like 'Find My Parking Cops' that have a high probability of being shut down by external authorities, viewing the short-lived experiment itself as a success.

**Communication Style:** His communication is direct, informal, and consistently self-deprecating. He actively avoids corporate or academic jargon, preferring to frame complex engineering feats in simple terms (e.g., 'I made a website'). When discussing his work, he downplays its political or ethical gravity, often redirecting conversations towards the technical challenge or the 'coolness' of the data. For example, regarding the parking app, he stated, 'I'm not 'pro' parking cop. I'm not 'anti' parking cop. It's just data I was able to unearth.' This pattern of framing his work as neutral experimentation is a key element of his public communication, allowing him to engage in provocative acts while maintaining a posture of detached curiosity.

**Likely Approach to New Projects:** Based on his patterns, a new project from Walz would likely originate from an observation of a quirky public data source or a nostalgic internet artifact. The development process would be swift and self-contained, resulting in a lightweight, single-purpose web application. The launch would be announced informally via his X account (@rtwlz), with the project hosted on his personal domain (walzr.com). He would likely author a brief, plain-language description of the project's function and his motivation, possibly with a nod to its ethical ambiguities but framed with playful intent (e.g., 'This is culture surveillance... It's about catching vibes'). He would engage with initial media attention but would likely resist being cast as an activist or artist, reiterating his identity as an engineer exploring an interesting dataset.

key_projects_summary.description: A website that reverse-engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system to display the last known location where a parking enforcement officer issued a ticket. The project went viral and the data source was disabled by the SFMTA within four hours of launch.

An art project described as 'culture surveillance' that uses a microphone placed in San Francisco's Mission District to constantly 'Shazam' and log the music being played on the street, creating a public feed of the neighborhood's 'vibes'.

A website that allows users to watch a random selection from millions of YouTube videos that have default camera filenames (e.g., 'IMG_0001.MOV') as their titles, surfacing unedited and often unseen moments of daily life.
A provocative project that cloned the Gmail interface to present publicly released emails associated with Jeffrey Epstein, framing the experience as if the user were logged into Epstein's account.
An AI-based project that rates attractiveness, which Walz himself describes as a 'mirror held up to our collective vanity' and acknowledges as being 'certainly biased' and 'flawed'.

A random route generator for runners and cyclists. Walz created the tool to help with his own summer training during high school, demonstrating an early example of building projects to solve personal needs.

content.media_portrayal_summary: Riley Walz is consistently portrayed in the media as a creative and mischievous figure at the intersection of engineering and art. Media outlets have applied several recurring labels to him, including the 'jester of Silicon Valley' (eWeek, TBPN podcast), a 'tech prankster' (Hoodline, The New York Times), and an 'internet artist' (The New Yorker). This characterization stems from his portfolio of projects that playfully subvert technology and expose public data in unexpected ways. For instance, his 'Find My Parking Cops' app was widely covered as a clever hack that, for a 'glorious morning' (WIRED), helped citizens avoid tickets before being shut down. His work is often framed as a form of social commentary, with The New Yorker describing him as 'The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online.' WIRED leans into the nostalgic aspect of his work, calling him 'The Guy Behind the Most Nostalgic Sites on the Internet' in reference to projects like IMG_0001. While Walz himself often downplays the significance of his work and resists labels, the media narrative casts him as a modern-day trickster who uses his technical skills to create viral moments and provoke thought, a reputation that was further solidified when his hiring by OpenAI was reported as the 'jester' joining a leading AI lab.
